We joined online by Jane Kelsey.
Morning, professor Kelsey, how are you? Excellent.
We've received your submission. You can take that as read.
Uh, we have 10 minutes allocated for your submission today,
and there may be questions
if you wish to leave time for that.
Thank you.
Uh, ki chair. Well, as you will know from the letters
that you've received,
I do not accept 10 minutes is the limit on
my submission,
and I give a heads up, uh, to the committee
that the ongoing refusal to take the
International Treaty examination as seriously as it needs
to be, is about to be escalated into
a much bigger discussion.
Um, I took the advantage of this
submission on the UAE, uh, to
remind the committee that in making submissions
as I have done for 30 plus years
of my international standing to analyze the agreement
and to challenge the national interest analysis prepared
by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs
and Trade to justify the agreement that NIA
as my submission shows, uh, is misleading in a number
of places, particularly pertinent
to my expertise in this instance, is that,
um, I was invited by, uh, Dr.
Kearney, the minister of, uh, state for foreign trade
from the United Arab Emirates, who was also responsible for,
uh, this agreement from the UAE side to be one
of six people, the others,
including a former director general
of the World Trade Organization to advise Dr.
Tanni on the UA e's hosting
of the WTO Ministerial Conference last year.
And I was invited to attend that conference
as his guest.
So this is not just a busy body
academic seeking to throw a spanner in the works.
I have deep knowledge
and recognized international expertise in this area
that the committee, um, needs to pay more heed to
because MFAs NIA is deeply problematic
in relation to this agreement.
Um, I've been particularly aware, uh,
that both the UAE
and the New Zealand Minister of Trade have
set as objectives securing as many agreements as possible
as quickly as possible.
The agreements therefore become a matter of optics
rather than a matter of substance.
And that is especially evident if we look at a number
of core aspects of, um, how MFA seeks
to justify the agreement.
If we look at the NIA, we see
the dilemmas when the
prescriptions are
largely uncontested.
Now, as, as Mai Solomon said, yes, uh,
there was a treaty assessment from
that is printed at the end of the NIA,
but that is not in any way influencing
MFA's thinking about this.
Part of the problem with this agreement was
that not only when negotiations conducted in secret,
there were cursory public consultations,
but those of us who did see the text in
that three month period
and have an opportunity
to input back on it under strict confidentiality in my case
as an advisor to we're not in fact able
to take those issues out to get advice
and feedback into the process.
And it is therefore not a process in itself
that can carry social license and legitimacy.
But if we look at some of the details of it, we, we see some
of the consequences of MFA writing the, the narrative.
And it was interesting that my old friend, Stephen Jacoby,
even admitted that there was very little in this agreement
commercially, uh, for them.
So if we are looking at the, the current 2023 level
of trade, uh, with the UAE,
we are looking at 1.1% of New Zealand's global exports.
If we are looking at the quantum overall as
MFA highlights of New Zealand's FTAs
that will now be covered, um, sorry, new Zealand's exports
that will now be covered by FTAs, that carries
with it an implication that FTAs somehow contribute to
economic growth and export growth.
Yet if we look at the data,
and I've been looking at the OECD data on this,
new Zealand's exports have actually fallen as a proportion
of GDP over recent years as we've been accumulating FTAs.
There is no direct correlation, it's there
as implied in the NIA.
Moreover, the areas that are identified as
of interest in relation to the UAE such as areas of
of red meat and dairy are areas
where we are already largely at saturation.
We are not gonna be expanding domestic production of those.
So that means that whatever might end up going
to the UAE is gonna be trade diversion, not trade creation.
If we go beyond that, the notion of tariff savings here
that could be 42.6 million is misleading in terms of
what those so-called tariff savings actually mean.
It's not money in the pocket of exporters.
It's potentially lower prices in the UAE if the tariff
cuts are in fact asked on to consumers.
And if consumers choice leads them to buy more
of New Zealand's exports, yet, not in the summary,
but down in page 84 of the NIA, we see the concession
that in fact the majority of New Zealand's exports
to the UAE are actually under a a duty
exemption program anyway.
So we've got a lot of smokes, smoke
and mirrors going on here.
We need to start looking much more distinctively at
what these agreements actually do
and do not do, how much resource we are putting into them
for what, and I note that, for example,
in the government procurement area, the UAE has has, uh,
reserved the right of a 10% premium to advantage.
Its SMEs. New Zealand doesn't have any equivalent of
that, that might actually provide some potential new

And we don't have that empirical data.
But if we are looking, say at Maori enterprises,
Maori enterprises, uh, are predominantly SMEs,
why can't we have similar kinds of, um,
advantages in our procurement?
Largely, I suspect
because we've already given away the right to do
that in the WTO gp, um, area, let me talk about,
uh, investment and services.
Um, as one who has a, a long history

Jacoby, I'm extremely pleased

in many international agreements.
The eu, for example, has just, uh,

of member state decisions to withdraw from, uh, several

as the green party, uh, members said, uh,
to chill governments by threatening

In, um, the investment chapter, it is disappointing

in the trade context.

requiring a certain amount of local content

uh, mills especially, and,
and FAT said, you got a problem there.
We've got issues as well in the services chapter.
Um, I raised the question around water and water bottling.
We'd finally got in fact to agree that the, um, commitments

which cover measures that effect foreign supply of services,
uh, as it related to water needed
to include in the protections, the right to constrain,
uh, the transfer, for example,
of water rights from an agriculture purpose
to a water bottling purpose.
It did that in the U-A-E-F-D-A.
It has not carried that through into this agreement.
If we'd had more time, we would have dealt with that.
Um, SPS sanitary and phyto sanitary measures
and technical barriers to trade.
Of course those are really important
to New Zealand agricultural exporters.
We know that. But at the same time, the kind of standards
that are being promoted, the foreclose, the ability
for example, of organics

and I say this as a num, one

about 40% of food in the UAE is genetically modified,
and that will include elements of
what they might export to New Zealand.
There is a provision in the gene technology bill for there

There are concerns not only about what that will mean here,
for example, for the organics exporters
and in the gene technology bill MB rejected, looking at

sorry, what the trade impacts of certification
of GE would mean for our exporters.
MFA said it should be a consideration. MB said no.
If we are looking at that in this context, we need

And if a future government decides to try to roll back some

the US when it tried to increase those protections.
And it lost and it lost not only on the general exceptions

which is actually stronger than the Treaty
of Wai exception in relation to digital.
Mr. Jacoby is wrong.
Most of our agreements are not unenforceable.
The only digital trade chapter

International treaty examination of the NZ – UAE Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement, and Agreement between the Government of New Zealand and the Government of the United Arab Emirates on the Promotion and Protection of Investments
